Title: Comment on RIN 0955-AA09
Abstract: Small and mid-sized independent practices rely on Medicare Advantage quality bonuses as essential financial buffers to afford the compliance and implementation costs of switching EHR systems, and reducing these bonuses will entrench existing vendo...
Comment:
Our clinic in Minnesota spent months last year evaluating a switch from Epic to a smaller vendor's EHR system. Their software was genuinely better for our workflow—faster charting, smarter alerts, fewer clicks. But the certification process and compliance costs were prohibitive. We're talking tens of thousands of dollars and a timeline we couldn't absorb. So we stayed with Epic, even though my staff remains frustrated we couldn't make the move.

Here's what concerns me about reducing Medicare Advantage quality bonuses: those bonuses are the only financial buffer that makes health IT transitions possible for independent practices like ours. When you cut that cushion, you're not promoting innovation. You're locking us in.

Small and mid-sized practices don't have the balance sheets of large health systems. We operate on thin margins. The quality bonuses we earn don't just fund staff bonuses—they fund infrastructure upgrades, training, and yes, the risk of trying new systems. Without them, the math doesn't work. We'll stay with whatever system we're on, no matter how outdated, because we literally cannot afford the compliance and implementation costs of switching.

Regarding streamlined testing requirements: real-world pilots are great in theory, but smaller vendors also need predictable pathways to certification that don't require vendors to bankroll expensive evidence-gathering. If you want competition to actually flourish, practices need stable reimbursement to take the financial risk of being early adopters.

Cutting bonuses doesn't reduce barriers to market entry. It entrenches them.

Title: Comment on RIN 0955-AA09
Abstract: Reducing certification barriers for health tech innovations is necessary but insufficient without concurrent preservation of Medicare Advantage quality bonuses that incentivize providers to adopt new solutions; without both deregulation and reimbu...
Comment:
Last year I sat in a meeting with a health tech founder who'd built something genuinely better—a maternal health app with superior UX, lower licensing costs, everything our provider clients were asking for. Within six months, they'd pivoted to Canada. Not because the product failed. They left because the certification pathway here was financially unworkable for a startup, and without a clear route to scale, the business case collapsed. That conversation has haunted me, because I realize how many solutions like that never even get a chance to compete in America.

I'm encouraged by the agency's push to reduce certification burden. Streamlining those requirements makes sense. But here's what worries me: we can't deregulate our way to innovation if we simultaneously defund the incentives that make providers willing to adopt new vendors in the first place.

Medicare Advantage quality bonuses are the mechanism that lets health systems actually invest in—and pay for—the innovations we're trying to unleash. When a provider can access bonus dollars, they have breathing room to pilot a new solution, to absorb implementation costs, to take a chance on a startup instead of playing it safe with incumbents. Cut those bonuses, and you cut the adoption dollars that make the business case viable for entrepreneurs.

My question is straightforward: How do we streamline real-world testing requirements while preserving the reimbursement incentives that make providers willing to take on certified innovations? We need both the deregulation *and* the market conditions that actually fund early adoption. Without that pairing, we're just making it easier for good solutions to leave.

Title: Comment on RIN 0955-AA09
Abstract: The commenter opposes loosening data security regulations for health information access, citing their daughter's identity theft experience involving a prescription tracking app, and argues that protecting consumer data should take priority over en...
Comment:
My daughter got signed up for one of those prescription tracking apps last year and within a couple weeks somebody used her information to charge stuff to her credit card. Three months. That's how long it took her to clean that mess up. So when I hear you all are talking about making it easier for companies to access people's health information and removing the rules that are supposed to keep that stuff safe, I have to speak up because this directly affects me and my family.

I don't understand why we need to loosen up on security just so tech companies can make more money. These aren't just numbers on a screen, they're our medical histories, our social security numbers, our financial information. If the government stops checking on whether these companies are actually protecting people's data, what's stopping the bad actors from getting in and stealing from us like they did to my daughter.

My Medicare Advantage plan works fine for me right now. I get my prescriptions, I see my doctors, things run smooth. I don't need some app company to make that "better" if it means loosening the safeguards that keep my information from getting stolen. That's not progress, that's just making it easier for somebody to commit fraud against regular working people like me.

Before you remove any of these certification requirements or change the rules about how companies can access and modify health data, you need to think about what happens to people when things go wrong. And they do go wrong. Ask my daughter.

Title: Comment on RIN 0955-AA09
Abstract: A caregiver supports improved health information sharing technology to manage his wife's diabetes care, but urges strong privacy protections against data breaches and warns against cutting Medicare Advantage quality bonus funding that currently en...
Comment:
My wife was diagnosed with diabetes about two years ago and since then I've been her caregiver, managing her doctor appointments, keeping track of her medications, carrying around this folder with all her test results and lab work to show different doctors. It would make things so much easier if I could just pull everything up on my phone instead of shuffling papers around and worrying about losing something important.

But here's what I'm really concerned about. The Medicare Advantage plan my wife is on has been helping us stay organized through their care coordination program. They have these digital tools that help me track her appointments and remind us when she needs refills. I know those programs are funded through quality bonus payments, and if those get cut back I'm worried the plan won't be able to afford keeping those tools and services running.

I'm all for making it easier to share health information, I really am. The technology side of things could definitely be better. But I'm also scared sick about her information ending up in the wrong hands. We're not exactly tech-savvy people and the thought of hackers getting into her diabetes records, her medications, her personal medical details—that keeps me up at night.

So please, before you loosen up all the privacy and security rules, make sure there are strong protections in place. And don't cut the funding that lets plans like ours provide the care management tools that actually help families like mine manage chronic illness. We need both better access and real privacy safeguards, not one or the other.
